rationale: This is a classic textbook example of the Ritter reaction. Tert-butanol is protonated by sulfuric acid and loses water to form the stable tert-butyl carbocation. This carbocation is then trapped by the nucleophilic nitrogen of acetonitrile, forming a nitrilium ion intermediate. Subsequent hydrolysis yields the final product, N-tert-butylacetamide. All components are simple, acyclic, and clearly illustrate the core transformation: carbocation formation, nitrile trapping, and hydrolysis to an amide.
collision_avoidance_note: This example uses simple, acyclic, and saturated starting materials (tert-butanol and acetonitrile) to avoid overlap with other named reactions. It avoids arenes (Friedel-Crafts), alkenes (hydroamination, Markovnikov additions), or other complex functional groups that could trigger unrelated reaction family classifications. The core transformation is clearly the formation of a C-N bond from a carbocation source and a nitrile, which is the defining feature of the Ritter reaction.
